draft.introduction: The Octavius of Minucius Felix (c. late 2nd century CE) is among the earliest Latin Christian apologetic works, structured as a philosophical dialogue between a Christian and a pagan on the beach at Ostia. Its literary sophistication rivals Cicero.

translation.text: Do you think we conceal what we worship because we have no temples and altars? What image of God shall I make, since, if you think rightly, man himself is the image of God? What temple shall I build for him, when this whole world, fashioned by his work, cannot contain him?
translation.original_text: An sine templis et aris non putatis nos colere quod colimus? Quod enim simulacrum Deo fingam, cum, si recte aestimes, sit Dei homo ipse simulacrum? Templum quod ei extruam, cum totus hic mundus eius opere fabricatus eum capere non possit?

translator_notes.note: The argument that “man himself is the image of God” (sit Dei homo ipse simulacrum) draws on Genesis 1:26–27 but is expressed in philosophical Latin that would resonate with Stoic and Platonic readers. Minucius avoids citing Scripture directly, preferring to argue from reason and classical authority.
